title: Commands
description: Define and configure your chat commands
summary:
Chat commands in Streamer.bot are **platform-agnostic**, supporting Twitch, YouTube and Kick.

Configuration options give you fine-grained control over command text parsing, cooldowns, and user permissions.

This tutorial was created using an older version of Streamer.bot. Some details may differ from the current version.

title: Get Command Group State
description: Get the current enabled state for all commands in a group

variables.name: commandGroupState
variables.description: Current enabled state for the command group

variables.name: commandsEnabled
variables.description: List of all commands currently *enabled* within this command group

variables.name: commandsDisabled
variables.description: List of all commands currently *disabled* within this command group

title: Get Command State
description: Check the current enabled state of a command
summary:
You could use this to check if a command is enabled or disabled. This value is stored in the Boolean variable commandState and can be True or False.

!get_command_state_dialog_box.png

Parameters

variables.name: commandState
variables.description: The current enabled state of this command

title: Get Commands
description: Fetch a list of all configured commands
summary:
This sub-action is useful if you want to output a list of commands to your chat.

The Get Commands sub-action will not include commands that have their location set to Anywhere

Handling Long Command Lists

parameters.name: Group
parameters.description: The command group to get commands from. Use "All" to get commands from all groups.

parameters.name: Variable Name
parameters.description: The variable name to store the resulting commands in.

parameters.name: Include All
parameters.description: Include all commands regardless of their `Include` option checked or not.

parameters.name: Only Has Permission
parameters.description: Only get commands for which the user has permissions.

parameters.name: Ignore Aliases
parameters.description: When checked it will only use the first command alias you have defined in the command settings.

variables.name: <variableName>
variables.description: Comma-separated string containing all commands matching your specified criteria

variables.name: <variableName>List
variables.description: C# List object containing all commands matching your specified criteria

title: Set Voice Control Command State
description: Voice Control Sub-Actions Reference
summary:
Overview
!sb-vc-state.png

To do this navigate to the Actions tab, next you can either create a new action or use an existing one.
Next in the Sub-Action's pane you will need to add this sub action so to do this we right-click in the Sub-Action pane next navigate the menus through the following Add Sub-Action the down the second menu to Voice Control the click on the Set Voice Control Command State action. Once selected a dialog box will appear like the one below.

!set-vc-state.png

title: Set Voice Control Command
description: Voice Control Sub-Actions Reference
summary:
Overview
With the Streamer.bot (0.18 +) you can now assign an action to a voice control command, then trigger this with a keyword or sentence that you say.
To do this you need to make sure you have streamer.bot listening to you on the correct input device. The microphone that you are going to use.

Pre-requirement
First open streamer.bot and then navigate the following tabs Voice Control then in the second row of tabs click Settings in this take provide you have a support speech recognition package installed it should be usable.
You will want the bot to start listening to you when you start the bot up so go ahead and enable the "Auto Start Listen" by checking the box just like the image below. Also in this tab on the bottom is the "Audio Input Device"
when you have done this be sure the bot is listening by click the **Start Listening** button. click Save button at the top.

!vc-setting-tab.png

title: Command Triggered
description: Trigger for when a Command is Triggered
summary:
Check out the Commands Guide for details on configuring your Commands

Parameters

Select a command from the Commands tab.

variables.name: command
variables.description: The command that was used

variables.name: commandId
variables.description: The ID of the command

variables.name: commandName
variables.description: The name of the command

variables.name: commandSource
variables.description: The command source

variables.name: commandType
variables.description: The type of the command

variables.name: msgId
variables.description: The unique message id for this message

variables.name: rawInput
variables.description: The message entered, if the command was a Starts With, this will be removed

variables.name: rawInputEscaped
variables.description: The message escaped

variables.name: rawInputUrlEncoded
variables.description: The message URL encoded

variables.name: input#
variables.description: The `#` word of the message entered, spaces are delimiters and variable names are 0 indexed, so `input0` would give the first word, `input1` would give the second, and so on

variables.name: inputEscaped#
variables.description: The indexed word escaped

variables.name: inputUrlEncoded#
variables.description: The indexed word URL encoded

variables.name: counter
variables.description: A running total of how many times a command has been run since application launch

variables.name: userCounter
variables.description: A running total of how many times the command has been run by current user since application launch

variables.name: match.count
variables.description:
Only when the command mode = "Regex"

The number of match groups (named or unnamed) in the regex.

Note that this is always 1 more than the number of parentheses groups, as the entire match is implicitly group 0.

variables.name: match[<index>]
variables.description:
Only when the command mode = "Regex"

The text matching each indexed capture group.

Index 0 is the entire matching text, and the first parenthesized group is 1.

variables.name: <groupName>
variables.description:
Only when the command mode = "Regex"

When using named capture groups (e.g, `(?'points'\d+)` or `(?<points>\d+)` ), each named capture group becomes an argument containing the text of the match.

title: Chatbot Commands
description: Configure some basic chat commands with Streamer.bot
summary:
Overview

If you are coming from another bot platform, you are probably used to having some basic commands available out of the box.

**Streamer.bot ships with a blank slate**, but a few simple actions can provide similar functionality.

title: AI Chat Command
description: Create a basic AI chat command with Ollama and Python
summary:
You can utilize the Run a Program sub-action to execute scripts with Streamer.bot :read-more{to="/api/sub-actions/core/system/run-a-program"} **Requires Streamer.bot v0.2.5-beta.2 or later**
A bug in previous versions of Streamer.bot prevented capturing of stdout from scripts

codeBlocks.label: Streamer.bot\data\scripts\ollama-example.py
codeBlocks.code:
import sys
    import ollama

    # Load the prompt from command line arguments
    prompt = " ".join(sys.argv[1:])

    # Generate text using llama3.1 model
    response = ollama.generate(model="llama3.1", prompt=prompt)

    # Print the response as a single line, truncated at 500 characters
    output = response["response"].replace("\n", " ")
    print(output[0:499])

title: Quotes Commands for v1.0.0+
description: An example how to get and add quotes using commands in Streamer.bot v1.0.0+
headings: Check whether input is empty or not
Get a random quote and send the message
Check whether input is a number or not
Get a specific quote and send the message
Check if the rawInput of the command is empty or not
Check if the quote was added successfully
Check whether input is empty or not
Check whether the quote was actually deleted by the input
summary:
This guide is only for **Streamer.bot v1.0.0** and above!

In Streamer.bot v1.0.0+, the built-in quote command "!quote" has been removed.
This allows for more customization and flexibility in how quotes are retrieved and displayed.
With that the "Show Quote" trigger will also not work anymore.

The following example shows how to:

title: Commands
name: Commands
description: Define and configure chat commands with Streamer.bot
summary:
Overview
!commands-018.png

This tab is used to view and define chat commands you want Streamer.bot to watch for and the actions each should perform.

<kbd>Right-click</kbd> anywhere in the pane to show the context menu. This will show diffrent options depending what was highlighted.

title: Quick Start - Chatbot Example
name: Quick Start - Chatbot Example
description: Learn how to configure some basic chat commands with Streamer.bot
summary:
Overview
If you are coming from another bot platform, you are probably used to having some basic commands available out of the box.

Streamer.bot ships with a blank slate - but a few simple actions can provide similar functionality.

Below, you will find a code to import them directly into your setup for immediate use and also explanations on why the workflows are set up this way so you can build more advanced actions if you want them.

title: Get Command Group State
name: Get Command Group State
description: Commands Sub-Actions Reference
summary:
Variables The following variables will be available after execution of this sub-action: Name | Description ----:|:------------ commandGroupState | Boolean for command group enabled state
True / False commandsEnabled | Dictionary of command ID, action IDs, of commands that are enabled commandsDisabled | Dictionary of command ID, action IDs, of commands that are disabled {.vars-table} ---

title: Get Command State
name: Get Command State
description: Commands Sub-Actions Reference
summary:
Overview
This will gather information on a single command's state.

You could use this to check if a command is enabled or disabled. This value is stored in the Boolean variable commandState and can be True or False.

!get_command_state_dialog_box.png

title: Get Commands
name: Get Commands
description: Commands Sub-Actions Reference
summary:
This sub action allows you to populate the commands and output them to your twitch chat. Just like the one below 
!get_all_commands_output_.png

To do this all you need to do is create an action and give it a name next go to the sub action window on the right and then right click to get the option menu to open now navigate through the following Add Action then Commands then click the Get Commands Option. Just like the image below. 
!commands_menu.png

Once you have done this a dialog box will appear from here you can select the group of commands you want to be outputted to the user that requested the commands list in your twitch chat. Various options will be displayed depending on how you have grouped your commands in the Commands tab.
> Note: Due to a limitation on Twitch if your total commands exceed 500 Characters the message to the Twitch chat will **NOT** be sent!!! 
{.is-info}

summary:
Overview

With this sub-action, you can run an exe, batch file, cmdlet, Powershell script, URI, or anything you can normally run via the <kbd>Win+R</kbd>, Windows Run dialog, or command prompt.

A common usage for this action, is using the LIV URI for switching camera profiles liv-app://camera/set/#

Overview
With the Streamer.bot (0.18 +) you can now assign an action to a voice control command, then trigger this with a keyword or sentence that you say.
To do this you need to make sure you have streamer.bot listening to you on the correct input device. The microphone that you are going to use.

Pre-requirement 
First open streamer.bot and then navigate the following tabs Voice Control then in the second row of tabs click Settings in this take provide you have a support speech recognition package installed it should be usable.
You will want the bot to start listening to you when you start the bot up so go ahead and enable the "Auto Start Listen" by checking the box just like the image below. Also in this tab on the bottom is the "Audio Input Device"
when you have done this be sure the bot is listening by click the **Start Listening** button. click Save button at the top.

!vc-setting-tab.png

title: Commands
name: Commands
description: TwitchSpeaker Commands Reference
summary:
List off all commands you can use in your Twitch chat:

Name | Description
----:|:------------
!tts status | Just gives a status of the current app state.
!tts voices | How many voices (and by type) are available.
!tts pause/resume | Pauses/resume speaking.
!tts clear | Well clear all pending phrases to be spoken.
!tts stop | Will immediately stop speaking if something is being said.
!tts mode [all Toggle between needing a command to speak and saying everything.
!tts commands | List all available custom commands.
!tts [off Clears, stops and disables speaking.
!tts [on Enables speaking.
!tts ignore [add Sets the ignore status of the specified user.
!tts ignored | List currently ignored users.
!tts reg [add Add or remove user from being a regular.
!tts events [on Toggle speaking of all events (subs, cheer, etc).
!tts random reset [username] | Resets a user back to there default voice, typically used if sticky random is enabled.
!tts set sticky [on Sets the sticky mode, if users are set to random, there first spoken voice will stick for the duration, unless reset using the above.
!tts assign last [username] | Will assign the last used voice to the user specified.
!tts [about Sends to chat the information about your TwitchSpeaker instance.

---

title: Custom Commands
name: Custom Commands
description: TwitchSpeaker Documentation
summary:
!overview.png

Custom commands let your Moderators, Subscribers, VIP's, Regulars and Everyone have a unique command/voice, this can be done be filling out a command in the Command field e.g. !say and for the Voice Alias field just simply choose one from the list and lastly you can limit this command to certain people so some can and others can't use it, here you can just modify everything like you want it.
